(died 664), also known as **Saint Tuda**, was appointed to succeed [Colman](/wiki/Colman_of_Lindisfarne "Colman of Lindisfarne") as [Bishop](/wiki/Bishop "Bishop") of [Lindisfarne](/wiki/Lindisfarne "Lindisfarne"). He served for less than a year. Although raised in [Ireland](/wiki/Ireland "Ireland"), he was a staunch supporter of [Roman](/wiki/Synod_of_Whitby#Background "Synod of Whitby") practices, being tonsured in the Roman manner and celebrating Easter according to the Roman [Computus](/wiki/Computus "Computus").[[1]](#cite_note-monks-1) However, he was consecrated as bishop in Ireland.[[2]](#cite_note-Kirby87-2)

He served for less than a year. From Wikipedia, the free encyclopedia

**Tuda of Lindisfarne** (died 664), also known as **Saint Tuda**, was appointed to succeed [Colman](/wiki/Colman_of_Lindisfarne "Colman of Lindisfarne") as [Bishop](/wiki/Bishop "Bishop") of [Lindisfarne](/wiki/Lindisfarne "Lindisfarne"). He served for less than a year. Although raised in [Ireland](/wiki/Ireland "Ireland"), he was a staunch supporter of [Roman](/wiki/Synod_of_Whitby#Background "Synod of Whitby") practices, being tonsured in the Roman manner and celebrating Easter according to the Roman [Computus](/wiki/Computus "Computus").[[1]](#cite_note-monks-1) However, he was consecrated as bishop in Ireland.[[2]](#cite_note-Kirby87-2)

## Life

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Tuda_of_Lindisfarne&action=edit&section=1 "Edit section: Life")]

Upon Colman's departure from Lindisfarne, he requested the king to appoint Abbot Eata of Melrose Abbey as his successor as Abbot of Lindisfarne. Tuda was appointed bishop of the Northumbrians.[[3]](#cite_note-miles-3)[[4]](#cite_note-catholic.org-4)[[5]](#cite_note-Harting111-5) Tuda had been educated in the south of Ireland.

Tuda became bishop in 664 and died in that same year.[[6]](#cite_note-Handbook219-6) The *[Anglo-Saxon Chronicle](/wiki/Anglo-Saxon_Chronicle "Anglo-Saxon Chronicle")* (in its entry for 656) includes a 664 charter for the minster of Medhamsted, or Peter-borough, which lists Tuda as among the consecrators.

The same *Chronicle* for the year 664 records that Tuda was one of many who died in the plague of that year.[[1]](#cite_note-monks-1)

Tuda's [feast day](/wiki/Feast_day "Feast day") is 21 October.[[4]](#cite_note-catholic.org-4)
